hero.title: CW1 e-portfolio: creating an evidence-based approach to identification
hero.tagline: This site is structured around the coursework brief so that each required competency area has its own evidence, reflection, and supporting material.

card.title: What this portfolio needs to prove
card.text: Use each section to show what you did, how you identified material, which tools or resources you used, and what you learned through reflection. Replace the prompts with your own notes, annotated images, records, and references.

sections.title: Aims and purpose
sections.subtitle: Use this opening section to explain what the coursework is for and how the site is organised.
sections.lead: The portfolio should function as evidence of identification competency rather than as a generic career website.

blocks.title: Course aims
items: Show understanding of the diversity of organisms being examined in the UK.
Create a useful resource for future work in ecology and conservation, initially focused on UK and EU contexts.
Develop links with people and organisations that can support progress as a proficient identifier and field ecologist.

blocks.title: How to frame the site
items: Introduce your approach to learning identification, the taxonomic groups you have worked on, and the types of evidence you have gathered across the module.
Make clear that each later section combines practical evidence with reflective writing so that the marker can see both what you did and what you learned.

blocks.title: Evidence model
items: Context: where the activity happened, why it was done, and which taxa or habitats were involved.
Activity: the process, methods, images, notes, or records that show you carrying out the work.
Reflection: what you learned, where uncertainty remained, and what you would improve with further practice.

sections.title: Competency 1: need for taxonomy and species identification
sections.subtitle: Show that you understand why taxonomy and accurate identification matter in ecology and conservation.
sections.lead: This section now reads as a complete coursework response rather than a prompt, and it can sit in the portfolio as a full draft for Competency 1.

blocks.title: Competency statement
items: Taxonomy and species identification are fundamental to ecology because ecological interpretation depends on knowing which organisms are present and how reliably they have been distinguished from similar taxa. Without accurate identification, records become less useful, monitoring results can be misleading, and management decisions may be based on incorrect assumptions about biodiversity value or ecological condition.

Through this module I have come to understand taxonomy not simply as a naming system, but as the framework that allows ecological observations to become meaningful evidence. Identification links field observation to data quality, conservation action, legal protection, and communication with other practitioners.

This competency therefore requires more than memorising names. It requires understanding why identification standards matter, recognising uncertainty, and selecting evidence and resources that justify an identification clearly and professionally.

blocks.title: Why taxonomy matters in ecological practice
items: Taxonomy provides the structure that allows organisms to be grouped, compared, and discussed consistently across surveys, reports, and datasets.
Species identification underpins ecological survey because habitat assessment, community interpretation, and species records all depend on recognising organisms correctly.
Reliable identification supports conservation decision-making, especially where protected, invasive, rare, or indicator species alter the significance of a site.
Accurate naming improves the value of records submitted to wider data systems, because poor identification weakens the quality of biodiversity evidence available to other users.
Identification skill also supports communication between ecologists, land managers, recorders, and statutory bodies by providing a common technical language.

blocks.title: Applied examples from ecological work
items: In field ecology, the need for accurate identification becomes obvious very quickly. A vegetation survey is only useful when the recorder can separate similar plant species and recognise which species are actually characteristic of the habitat being assessed. In the same way, invertebrate or vertebrate observations only become meaningful when the observer can justify why a record belongs to one taxon rather than another.

This matters because ecological conclusions often depend on comparatively small differences. Misidentifying a species could affect habitat condition assessment, produce false distribution data, or lead to overconfidence in a record that should have remained provisional. I now see identification as a core evidence skill rather than a background task.

The module has also shown me that taxonomy is directly connected to practical workflow. The process starts before the name is assigned: observing the specimen carefully, selecting useful characters, checking the right resource, considering confusion species, and only then arriving at a defensible conclusion.

blocks.title: My developing understanding
items: At the start of the module I understood species identification in a fairly general sense, but I had not fully appreciated how much discipline it requires. I initially thought the main challenge was remembering characteristics, whereas in practice the more difficult task is learning to observe systematically and to justify why a character is diagnostic rather than simply noticeable.

As I gained more experience, I became more aware of uncertainty. This has been an important shift in my thinking. Competent identification is not pretending to know more than I do; it is being able to recognise when evidence is strong, when an identification should remain tentative, and when further checking is required using another specimen feature, another image, or a better key.

This change in approach has made me more careful and more reflective. I now understand that good identification is inseparable from good note-taking, image quality, and the honest reporting of confidence level.

blocks.title: Evidence I should include on this page
items: Annotated field or lab photographs showing the characters used to separate taxa.
Short case examples where identification affected ecological interpretation or record quality.
Notes showing which keys, guides, or digital resources were consulted.
Examples of uncertainty, including confusion species and how the final conclusion was reached or left open.
A brief reflective commentary in my own voice explaining what improved through practice.

blocks.title: Reflection
items: The main insight I have gained from Competency 1 is that taxonomy gives structure to ecological knowledge, while identification turns that structure into usable evidence. Before carrying out identification tasks myself, I underestimated how much judgement is involved in moving from observation to conclusion.

I have also learned that confidence should be earned rather than assumed. The strongest identifications are the ones that can be explained clearly with reference to observed characters and appropriate resources. This is the standard I need to keep developing in the later competency sections of the portfolio.

Going forward, I want to improve my speed without lowering my standard of evidence. At present I am becoming more methodical, but I still need more repetition across different taxa so that careful identification becomes both accurate and efficient under field conditions.

sections.title: Competency 2: specimen handling and curation
sections.subtitle: Document how specimens were handled, preserved, labelled, mounted, stored, or photographed.
sections.lead: This section now reads as a complete coursework response for Competency 2 and is ready to be supported with your own specimen images, notes, and examples of technique.

blocks.title: Competency statement
items: Specimen handling and curation are essential parts of identification because the quality of the specimen, image, or preserved material directly affects how confidently diagnostic characters can be observed. Even strong taxonomic knowledge is less useful if the specimen has been damaged, stored poorly, labelled inadequately, or presented without enough supporting information.

Through this module I have understood that handling and curation are not separate from identification. They are part of the same chain of evidence. Good practice allows later checking, comparison, and justification, while poor practice can remove or obscure the very characters needed to make a reliable identification.
For this reason, specimen handling should be approached as a technical skill that requires care, consistency, and attention to detail rather than as a minor preparation step.

blocks.title: Why handling and curation matter
items: They protect important morphological characters from damage during collection, transport, observation, and storage.
They make later re-examination possible, which is especially important when confidence is low or when verification by another person is needed.
They improve traceability by connecting the specimen or image to date, location, habitat, and collection context.
They support professional standards because a well-presented specimen or evidence record is easier to interpret and defend.
They encourage a more disciplined approach to observation, labelling, and note-taking.

blocks.title: What I have learned in practice
items: My understanding of specimen handling has become much more practical through experience. At first, I mainly thought about getting a specimen or image quickly enough to identify it. As I worked through activities, I became more aware that how the material is handled from the beginning affects everything that follows, including visibility of characters, confidence of identification, and whether the evidence is useful to someone else.

I have learned that careful handling involves slowing down and thinking about the condition of the material before trying to name it. This includes considering whether the specimen is intact, whether key features are visible, whether it needs to be positioned differently for photography or examination, and whether enough contextual information has been recorded alongside it.

I also now recognise that curation is part of communication. A well-labelled and well-presented specimen, image set, or record makes the reasoning behind an identification much clearer to another reader or assessor.

blocks.title: Good practice to demonstrate
items: Clear labelling with date, location, habitat, and any other relevant collection details.
Careful positioning or mounting so important structures can be seen clearly.
Photography that captures more than one view where a single angle is not enough.
Storage or preservation choices that reduce damage and maintain useful features.
Consistent recording of observations so that the specimen can still be interpreted later.

blocks.title: Difficulties and limitations
items: One of the main challenges is that good handling requires both technical knowledge and patience. It is easy to focus too quickly on naming the organism and not enough on the preparation and presentation of the evidence. This can lead to missing structures, low-quality photographs, incomplete labels, or uncertainty that could have been reduced earlier in the process.

Another difficulty is that different taxa and specimen sizes require different handling approaches. A method that works for a larger or more robust specimen may not be suitable for delicate or very small material. This means technique needs to be adapted rather than applied automatically.

I am still developing confidence in knowing when my evidence is strong enough and when I need to improve the image, reposition the material, or record more contextual information before treating the identification as secure.

blocks.title: Evidence I should include on this page
items: Images showing the specimen before, during, or after handling or preparation.
Examples of labels, storage notes, or collection records.
Photographs that show why one view was insufficient and why extra views were needed.
Short commentary explaining how handling affected identification quality.
A reflective note on what I would now do differently after gaining more experience.

blocks.title: Reflection
items: The main lesson I have taken from Competency 2 is that specimen handling and curation are evidence skills as much as technical tasks. They determine whether identification can be checked, explained, and trusted.

This has made me more aware of how professional ecological work depends not only on recognising species, but also on presenting observations in a way that retains their scientific value. Careful handling is therefore part of producing credible ecological evidence.

Going forward, I need more repetition so that good handling practice becomes automatic. I want to improve my consistency in labelling, image planning, and the presentation of material so that my later identification work is stronger and easier to defend.

sections.title: Competency 3: taxa identification
sections.subtitle: Split this into plants, invertebrates, and vertebrates so the marker can see coverage clearly.
sections.lead: This section now reads as a complete coursework response for Competency 3 and is structured around the required taxonomic coverage of plants, invertebrates, and vertebrates.

blocks.title: Competency statement
items: Taxa identification is a central ecological skill because the ability to distinguish plants, invertebrates, and vertebrates accurately determines the quality of field records, habitat interpretation, and ecological reporting. This competency requires more than recognising familiar organisms. It involves observing diagnostic characters carefully, selecting appropriate resources, comparing similar taxa, and making a judgement that can be justified with evidence.

Through this module I have come to understand that identification is a process of reasoning rather than a simple act of recall. A name should follow from observed evidence, not from first impressions. This means the strongest identification work includes clear signposting between what was seen, which features were used, and why those features support one taxon over another.

Competency 3 is therefore best shown by combining taxonomic coverage with reflective commentary. For each group, I need to demonstrate the characters used, the limits of certainty, the tools consulted, and how my confidence developed through practice.

blocks.title: Plants
items: Plant identification has shown me the importance of looking beyond general appearance. Early on, it was tempting to rely on overall form or colour, but I have learned that a more defensible approach requires attention to features such as leaf arrangement, venation, margins, flower structure, stem characteristics, growth habit, and habitat context. This has made plant identification feel both more technical and more reliable.

One of the major lessons from plant work is that a single photograph is often not enough. Different parts of the plant may need to be documented separately, especially where diagnostic characters are distributed across leaves, flowers, stems, or fruits. Because of this, I now understand why well-structured evidence matters so much when proving identification.

My confidence with plants has improved through repeated comparison of similar species and through using keys and guides more methodically. However, I still need further practice in recognising which characters are truly diagnostic and which are only generally helpful.

blocks.title: Invertebrates
items: Invertebrate identification has made it especially clear that observation quality is critical. Diagnostic structures can be small, hidden, or easily damaged, which means that close examination, careful photography, and occasionally a more cautious identification level are often necessary. This has taught me that responsible identification sometimes means stopping at a level that the evidence genuinely supports.

Compared with some vertebrate work, invertebrate identification often feels more demanding because fine morphological detail matters more and because confusion between similar taxa can be difficult to resolve quickly. As a result, I have learned to slow down, use multiple features where possible, and avoid becoming overconfident based on one visible trait alone.

This part of the module has strengthened my appreciation of taxonomic keys and close descriptive language. It has also shown me that good curation and image quality are directly linked to successful identification in groups where diagnostic traits are subtle.

blocks.title: Vertebrates
items: Vertebrate identification often depends on a different balance of evidence. In some cases, visible field marks are sufficient, but behaviour, silhouette, vocalisation, habitat, and viewing conditions can also become important. This has shown me that identification is always contextual and that the right characters depend on the taxonomic group and the encounter itself.

I have learned that vertebrate records can appear easier because the organisms are often more familiar, but this can create a false sense of certainty. Similar species, poor light, distance, or short observations can all reduce reliability. For this reason, I now place more value on recording exactly what I observed rather than simply writing the species name immediately.
This has improved the way I think about evidence. A credible vertebrate identification should still be tied to observed features and not rely only on assumption or familiarity.

blocks.title: What this competency should evidence
items: Coverage of plants, invertebrates, and vertebrates, including taxa addressed during the course.
Images or labelled figures showing the characters used in each identification.
Brief notes on which keys, apps, guides, or online tools were consulted.
Recognition of confusion species or reasons for uncertainty.
Reflection on how confidence changed through repeated practice.

blocks.title: Reflection
items: The most important lesson from Competency 3 is that identification skill develops through repeated observation, comparison, and honest assessment of certainty. I have become more aware that the real challenge is not simply knowing names, but learning how to justify them clearly and responsibly.

I have also learned that different taxonomic groups demand different habits of attention. Plant work often requires structured comparison across several body parts, invertebrate work demands close attention to fine detail, and vertebrate work often depends on combining visible characters with context and behaviour.

Going forward, I want to improve both breadth and depth. I need broader familiarity across more taxa, but I also need to become quicker at recognising which characters are most useful for making efficient and defensible identifications in the field.

sections.title: Competency 4: sampling and rapid assessment techniques
sections.subtitle: Focus on methods where identification was part of the workflow under real field conditions.
sections.lead: This section now reads as a complete coursework response for Competency 4 and focuses on sampling and rapid assessment techniques that required identification in practice.

blocks.title: Competency statement
items: Sampling and rapid assessment techniques are important because identification in ecology rarely happens only under ideal conditions. In practice, organisms often need to be identified while time is limited, weather is variable, specimens are moving or incomplete, and survey methods impose their own constraints. Competency in this area therefore means being able to combine field method with taxonomic judgement.

This part of the module has shown me that identification skill changes when it is embedded within sampling. The challenge is no longer just whether I can identify something eventually, but whether I can identify it accurately enough within the pace and structure of a real method such as a transect, quadrat, sweep net sample, or rapid habitat assessment.
Competency 4 therefore requires evidence that I can connect sampling design, field observation, and identification quality in a realistic and reflective way.

blocks.title: Why identification matters during sampling
items: Rapid ecological methods depend on quick but defensible identification decisions.
Errors made during sampling can affect abundance estimates, species lists, and later interpretation of site quality or community structure.
Field conditions often reduce the time available for checking, so method and identification skill have to work together.
Some records can be identified confidently on the spot, while others require later confirmation, and recognising that difference is part of competent practice.
Sampling also teaches efficiency by forcing attention onto the characters that matter most in the moment.

blocks.title: What I have learned from rapid assessment work
items: Working within sampling methods has made identification feel more applied and more demanding. In a classroom or independent setting, there is often more time to revisit a guide, compare options, and think slowly. During field sampling, decisions need to be made in a more structured and time-limited way, which changes the kind of attention required.

This has taught me that speed and accuracy have to be balanced carefully. The goal is not to rush towards a name, but to recognise what level of certainty is realistic within the method being used. In some cases, this means identifying confidently in the field; in others, it means recording carefully and deferring final confirmation until the specimen, image, or notes can be checked properly.

I have also learned that repeated use of the same sampling method improves efficiency. Familiarity with the workflow makes it easier to focus on the most useful characters and reduces the time lost through uncertainty or disorganisation.

blocks.title: Examples of methods to discuss
items: Quadrats and vegetation assessment.
Transects and rapid habitat recording.
Sweep netting or other invertebrate collection methods.
Kick sampling or freshwater rapid assessment where relevant.
Point counts or other vertebrate observation methods where identification happened in real time.

blocks.title: Key difficulties in field-based identification
items: The main difficulty is that field conditions introduce uncertainty in ways that are not always present in slower identification work. Weather, light, movement, distance, specimen condition, and time pressure can all affect what is visible and how confident an identification can be.

Another challenge is maintaining consistent recording standards while still keeping up with the pace of the method. It is easy to focus on completing the survey and not enough on preserving the evidence needed to support later interpretation.

These difficulties have made me more aware that competent sampling is not just technical efficiency. It also requires judgement about when to confirm, when to record provisionally, and when to revisit uncertain material later.

blocks.title: Evidence I should include on this page
items: Images of sampling in progress.
Field notes, tally sheets, or habitat recording forms.
Examples showing what was identified immediately and what required later checking.
Short commentary on how field conditions affected the method and the identification process.
Reflection on how repetition improved efficiency or confidence.

blocks.title: Reflection
items: The main insight I have gained from Competency 4 is that identification becomes more demanding, but also more meaningful, when it is embedded in real sampling situations. It is in those situations that observation, method, and judgement have to work together.

I have become more aware of the difference between being able to identify a specimen eventually and being able to work effectively within the pace of field ecology. This has encouraged me to think more carefully about evidence quality, confidence level, and when follow-up checking is necessary.

Going forward, I want to improve my ability to make efficient decisions under field conditions without lowering the standard of evidence behind them. More repetition with structured methods will be important for building that confidence.

sections.title: Competency 5: wider community engagement
sections.subtitle: Include evidence of continued professional development and engagement beyond core practicals.
sections.lead: This section now reads as a complete coursework response for Competency 5 and is designed to be populated with your own evidence from courses, workshops, volunteering, and other professional engagement.

blocks.title: Competency statement
items: Wider community engagement is an important part of identification training because taxonomic skill develops through repeated exposure, practice, and contact with knowledgeable people. Identification cannot be mastered from lectures alone. It improves through participation in recording communities, practical events, specialist training, volunteering, and opportunities to learn from more experienced practitioners.

This competency has helped me understand that professional development in ecology is not only about gaining information, but also about entering the communities where identification knowledge is shared, tested, and refined. These forms of engagement expose me to good practice, specialist language, and realistic expectations of the standard required in field work and recording.

For this reason, Competency 5 is not an optional extra. It shows whether I am actively building the habits, networks, and practical experience that support long-term development as an identifier and field ecologist.

blocks.title: Why wider engagement matters
items: It provides access to experienced recorders, ecologists, and specialists who can model good identification practice.
It exposes me to taxa, methods, and resources that may not be covered fully in taught sessions.
It helps build confidence through repeated practice in real settings rather than only controlled teaching environments.
It supports professional growth by linking technical skill with the wider ecology and conservation community.
It encourages reflective learning by showing how others approach uncertainty, verification, and evidence standards.

blocks.title: What counts as valid engagement
items: Courses, whether online or in person, that include identification content or relevant field methods.
Recording scheme days and events run by local or national recording groups.
Specialist workshops, society field days, museum sessions, or organisation-led practical events.
Volunteering that involves habitat work, specimen work, survey support, or other identification-related tasks.
Presentations and seminar series with a meaningful species identification or sampling component.

blocks.title: What I have learned from this competency
items: The most important idea I have taken from this competency is that identification skill is social as well as technical. Although individual practice matters, progress is much faster when I am exposed to people who can explain their reasoning, highlight overlooked characters, and demonstrate how to work to professional standards.

I have also learned that these activities build judgement as much as knowledge. By seeing how experienced people deal with uncertainty, verify records, and choose resources, I have begun to understand identification as a disciplined practice rather than a simple memory task.

This has changed how I think about development. Instead of treating external activities as extra enrichment, I now see them as part of the core process of becoming more reliable and more employable in ecology.

blocks.title: How I should evidence each activity
items: State what the activity was and who organised it.
Explain what I observed, practised, or learned.
Include imagery, notes, certificates, screenshots, or records where available.
Add a reflective comment on the usefulness of the activity relative to my own interests and skill level.
Show how the activity contributed to identification competence rather than simply listing attendance.

blocks.title: Reflection
items: Competency 5 has made me more conscious that progress in taxonomy and identification depends on continued engagement beyond the minimum course requirements. It is through these repeated interactions and activities that identification begins to feel like part of a professional practice rather than a classroom exercise.

I also recognise that I still need to broaden my exposure to different taxa and specialist communities. My next step should be to keep adding evidence from events and activities that push me beyond the groups or methods I already find familiar.
Overall, this competency has strengthened my understanding that becoming a proficient identifier requires commitment, curiosity, and participation in the wider ecology community.

sections.title: Tools and strategies for identification
sections.subtitle: Show the tools you used and critique them briefly instead of just listing them.
sections.lead: This section now reads as a complete coursework response for tools and strategies, and it can be expanded with your own screenshots, comparisons, and critiques of the resources you used.

blocks.title: Purpose of this section
items: Identification depends not only on what I know, but also on how effectively I use the tools available to me. Different resources support different stages of the process, from planning where to search for species, to narrowing down likely taxa, to confirming diagnostic characters and recording the final observation properly.

This section therefore acts as both evidence of practice and a developing resource bank for future ecological work. By reflecting on the strengths and limitations of each tool, I can show that I am not just collecting resources, but learning how to use them critically.

blocks.title: Locating species
items: Tools such as NBN Atlas and GBIF are useful at the planning stage because they show species distributions and help identify where taxa are likely to be found. This makes them valuable for preparing fieldwork, selecting survey locations, and understanding whether a record is plausible in a given area.

The main lesson I have learned from using range information is that distribution data should inform field expectations without replacing direct observation. A species may be likely in an area, but the final identification must still be based on the specimen or field evidence rather than assumed from location alone.

blocks.title: Keys, guides, and digital resources
items: Taxonomic keys are useful for structured decision-making because they force attention onto diagnostic characters rather than general impression.
Field guides are helpful for quick comparison, orientation, and visual familiarity, especially in the early stages of learning a taxonomic group.
Apps and online resources can improve speed and accessibility, but they still need to be checked critically rather than trusted automatically.
Museum collections, expert communities, and specialist websites provide depth when common guides are not sufficient for difficult or similar taxa.
Optical tools such as hand lenses or microscopes can be essential where important features are too small to assess confidently with the naked eye.

blocks.title: How I should critique each tool
items: For each resource, I should explain how I used it, what stage of the identification process it supported, and how reliable it felt in practice. A strong portfolio entry should not just state that a key or app was used; it should explain why that resource was appropriate and what limitations became clear during use.

This is important because no tool is universally best. Some are better for beginners, some are better for confirming detail, and some are better for quick orientation in the field. Demonstrating awareness of these differences shows developing professional judgement.

blocks.title: What I have learned from this section
items: The main thing I have learned is that identification becomes more reliable when resources are combined rather than used in isolation. A guide may help narrow the possibilities, a key may test the diagnostic features, and a recording resource may help assess whether the result is plausible within the local context.

I have also become more aware that tools do not replace observation. Good resources support attention and reasoning, but they cannot compensate for poor-quality evidence or careless interpretation. This has made me more careful about checking characters directly and using tools to support, rather than substitute for, judgement.

blocks.title: Reflection
items: This section has encouraged me to think more strategically about how I identify organisms. Instead of reaching for a familiar resource automatically, I now try to consider which tool is most appropriate for the taxon, the evidence available, and the level of confidence required.

Going forward, I want to build a more deliberate toolkit for different taxonomic groups and become more fluent in switching between planning tools, field resources, and confirmatory resources. That will make my identification process more efficient and more defensible.

sections.title: iRecord activity
sections.subtitle: Include evidence that you practised biological recording using iRecord.
sections.lead: This section now reads as a complete coursework response for the iRecord requirement and is ready for you to add screenshots, record examples, and reflective commentary from your own submissions.

blocks.title: Competency statement
items: Using iRecord is an important part of this portfolio because it connects identification practice to real biological recording. It shows that species observations are not only being recognised for coursework purposes, but are being considered as records that may contribute to wider biodiversity data systems when supported by appropriate evidence.

This activity has helped me understand that recording is part of professional ecological practice. A record must be clear, appropriately evidenced, and responsibly submitted. This means that identification, metadata, image quality, and confidence are all linked when using a system such as iRecord.
The iRecord component therefore demonstrates how field identification can be translated into a format that is useful beyond the immediate learning exercise.

blocks.title: What iRecord practice has taught me
items: The most useful lesson from this activity is that recording makes me think more carefully about evidence quality. When submitting a record, it is not enough to feel that an identification is probably right. I need to consider whether the observation is supported by enough information for another person to interpret or verify it.

This has encouraged better habits in the field and afterwards. I now pay more attention to the quality of images, the completeness of location details, and whether the record includes enough context to be meaningful. In that sense, iRecord has strengthened both my identification discipline and my data recording discipline.

blocks.title: What should be shown on this page
items: Screenshots or examples of records submitted through iRecord.
Images used to support the submitted records.
Short notes on the species, location, date, and why the record was credible.
Any verifier comments, corrections, or follow-up learning where relevant.
A reflection on how recording practice changed the way I collect evidence.

blocks.title: Why iRecord matters for ecology
items: iRecord matters because it links individual observations to wider ecological data use. It reinforces that records should be accurate, supported, and useful to others rather than kept only as personal notes.

This makes the exercise valuable beyond the module itself. It encourages me to think like a practitioner contributing to biological recording standards and not just like a student completing an assignment.

blocks.title: Reflection
items: The iRecord activity has made me more aware that recording is a form of accountability. Once an observation is entered into a recording workflow, the quality of the evidence becomes much more important, because the record may be used by others in ways that extend beyond my own learning.

I have also learned that careful recording improves identification habits. Knowing that a record may need to stand up to checking encourages me to observe more carefully, document more clearly, and be more honest about uncertainty.
Going forward, I want to continue using recording platforms as part of my development, because they provide a practical bridge between identification practice and professional ecological data use.

sections.title: Format and rubric checklist
sections.subtitle: Use this final section as a build checklist before submission.
sections.lead: This section now reads as a finished submission checklist based on the coursework brief and rubric, so it can stay visible while you populate the portfolio with your own evidence.

blocks.title: Formatting and submission requirements
items: The assessment is submitted via a website.
The writing should remain readable and professionally presented throughout.
References should be given in Harvard style where required.
The overall presentation should support accessibility, navigation, and clear signposting.

blocks.title: What the marker is looking for
items: Completeness: all requested elements are covered clearly.
Presentation and creativity: the site is accessible, intuitive, and well structured.
Insights and reflection: the writing uses my own voice and demonstrates reflective depth.
Evidence: claims of competency are supported convincingly by notes, images, records, and explanation.
Style: the language is professional, domain-specific, grammatically careful, and appropriate for ecology and conservation.

blocks.title: Checks for each section
items: Does the section begin with a clear explanation of purpose?
Does it include context, activity, and reflection rather than only description?
Are images signposted and interpreted rather than left unexplained?
Are identification claims linked clearly to characters, evidence, or resources used?
Can an independent reader understand why the material proves competency?

blocks.title: Writing and presentation reminders
items: The portfolio should sound personal and authoritative rather than copied from general sources. The strongest sections will be the ones where my own reasoning and learning are visible in the writing.

I should keep the tone professional, use British English, proofread carefully, and prefer accurate technical vocabulary over vague wording. Images from other sources should be acknowledged appropriately, and any borrowed material should be used carefully and referenced correctly.

blocks.title: Final reflection before submission
items: The final stage of building this portfolio is not only checking whether all sections are present, but also asking whether the evidence genuinely proves developing competence. A complete portfolio should do more than display material; it should persuade the reader that I understand identification as a practical, reflective, and evidence-based skill.
Using this checklist helps ensure that the website remains aligned with the coursework brief while still allowing room for personality, creativity, and clear professional communication.
